hydra_tod
  • MCMC Diagnostics
  • View page source

MCMC Diagnostics

This module provides standard MCMC convergence diagnostics for assessing the quality of posterior samples returned by the Gibbs sampler.

The typical workflow after collecting samples is:

from hydra_tod.mcmc_diagnostics import diagnostics

# samples shape: (n_chains, n_iterations, n_params)
summary = diagnostics(samples, param_names=["logf0", "alpha"])
# summary["logf0"] -> {"ESS_min": ..., "ESS_median": ..., "Rhat_split": ...}

The three key quantities are:

ess_1d()

Effective Sample Size (ESS) — estimates the number of effectively independent samples accounting for autocorrelation. Values well below n_iterations indicate poor mixing.

rhat_split()

Split-:math:`hat{R}` — inter-chain convergence diagnostic. Values close to 1.0 (< 1.01 is a common threshold) indicate that all chains have converged to the same distribution.

acf_1d()

Autocorrelation function (ACF) — FFT-based computation up to a specified maximum lag. Useful for choosing the thinning interval.

diagnostics()

Convenience wrapper that computes ESS and split-\(\hat{R}\) for all parameters and optionally produces trace and ACF plots.

hydra_tod.mcmc_diagnostics.acf_1d(x: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], max_lag: int = 200) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]][source]

Compute the normalised autocorrelation function (ACF) of a 1-D series.

Uses the FFT-based method for efficient computation of the ACF up to max_lag.

Parameters:

  • x (NDArray[np.floating]) – Input time series of shape (n,).

  • max_lag (int, optional) – Maximum lag to compute. Default is 200.

Returns:

acf – Normalised ACF values from lag 0 to max_lag (inclusive), shape (max_lag + 1,).

Return type:

NDArray[np.floating]

Notes

The ACF is normalised by the variance and the number of overlapping samples at each lag.

hydra_tod.mcmc_diagnostics.ess_1d(x: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]]) → float[source]

Estimate the effective sample size (ESS) of a 1-D MCMC chain.

Computes the ESS using the initial-positive-sequence estimator: the ACF is summed until the first non-positive value is encountered.

Parameters:

x (NDArray[np.floating]) – MCMC chain of shape (n,).

Returns:

ess – Estimated effective sample size.

Return type:

float

Notes

If the variance of x is zero (constant chain), returns n directly.

hydra_tod.mcmc_diagnostics.rhat_split(chains_dim: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]]) → float[source]

Compute the split-\(\hat{R}\) convergence diagnostic.

Each chain is split in half, doubling the number of chains and halving their length, before computing the standard \(\hat{R}\) statistic.

Parameters:

chains_dim (NDArray[np.floating]) – Array of shape (n_chains, n_draws) for a single parameter dimension.

Returns:

Rhat – Split-\(\hat{R}\) value. Values close to 1.0 indicate convergence; values above ~1.01 suggest the chains have not mixed.

Return type:

float

References

Gelman, A. et al. (2013), Bayesian Data Analysis, 3rd edition.

hydra_tod.mcmc_diagnostics.diagnostics(samples: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], param_names: list[str] | None = None, max_plots: int = 3) → dict[str, dict[str, float]][source]

Compute and display MCMC convergence diagnostics.

For each parameter, computes the minimum and median effective sample size (ESS) across chains and the split-\(\hat{R}\). Optionally plots trace plots for the first few parameters.

Parameters:

  • samples (NDArray[np.floating]) – MCMC samples of shape (n_chains, n_draws, n_params).

  • param_names (list of str or None, optional) – Names for each parameter dimension. If None, defaults to ['param0', 'param1', ...].

  • max_plots (int, optional) – Maximum number of parameters to produce trace plots for. Default is 3.

Returns:

summary – Dictionary keyed by parameter name, each containing:

  • 'ESS_min' : minimum ESS across chains.

  • 'ESS_median' : median ESS across chains.

  • 'Rhat_split' : split-Rhat value.

Return type:

dict
